parts.label: Nose Cone
parts.realLocation: At the very top of the rocket. (Typical hobby sounding rocket; exact shapes vary by model.)
parts.why: Cuts through the air smoothly and often holds the recovery system.

parts.label: Flight Computer
parts.realLocation: Just below the nose cone, in the recovery bay. (Typical layout; exact position varies by model.)
parts.why: Tracks altitude and fires the parachute at the right moment.

parts.label: Igniter Battery
parts.realLocation: Next to the flight computer in the electronics bay.
parts.why: Powers the flight computer and lights the motor igniter.

parts.label: Parachute
parts.realLocation: Packed just below the nose cone, ready to eject upward.
parts.why: Slows the rocket down so it lands safely and can fly again.

parts.label: Backup Streamer
parts.realLocation: Packed alongside the parachute as a backup.
parts.why: Adds extra drag if the main parachute doesn't open.

parts.label: Payload Bay
parts.realLocation: A hollow section in the middle of the body tube.
parts.why: Carries a camera, experiment, or tracking beacon.

parts.label: Body Tube (Airframe)
parts.realLocation: The long tube that makes up most of the rocket's length.
parts.why: Holds every part together and gives the rocket its shape.

parts.label: Launch Rail Guide
parts.realLocation: Glued to the outside of the body tube.
parts.why: Slides along the launch rail to keep the rocket going straight at liftoff.

parts.label: Rocket Motor
parts.realLocation: Inside the motor mount near the bottom of the rocket.
parts.why: Burns propellant to create the thrust that lifts the rocket.

parts.label: Solid Propellant
parts.realLocation: Packed inside the motor casing itself.
parts.why: The fuel that actually burns to produce thrust.

parts.label: Motor Igniter
parts.realLocation: Inserted into the bottom of the motor.
parts.why: A small electric spark that lights the propellant at launch.

parts.label: Fin Set
parts.realLocation: Attached to the outside at the very bottom of the body tube.
parts.why: Keeps the rocket flying straight, like feathers on a dart.

parts.label: Telemetry Transmitter
parts.realLocation: Mounted in the electronics bay near the flight computer, with a small antenna poking through the body tube. (Typical hobby rocket layout; exact position varies by model.)
parts.why: Sends live altitude, speed, and location data down to the ground team during flight.

parts.label: Camera Pod
parts.realLocation: Clipped to the side of the body tube, usually just below the electronics bay, with its lens facing outward. (Typical hobby rocket layout; exact position varies by model.)
parts.why: Films the flight so you can watch liftoff, staging, and the view from the sky afterward.

parts.label: Tracking Beacon
parts.realLocation: Tucked inside the body tube near the recovery bay, wired to its own small battery. (Typical hobby rocket layout; exact position varies by model.)
parts.why: Sends out a radio signal so you can find your rocket after it lands, even in tall grass or trees.

parts.label: Payload Fairing / Bay
parts.realLocation: At the very top, protecting the payload during launch.
parts.why: Shields the satellite or cargo from air resistance and heat.

parts.label: Solar Panel
parts.realLocation: Folded against the payload before launch, unfolds in space.
parts.why: Generates electrical power for the satellite payload.

parts.label: Nose Fairing
parts.realLocation: The pointed cover just below the payload bay.
parts.why: Cuts through the air with minimum drag during ascent.

parts.label: Guidance System
parts.realLocation: In the interstage section, between the two stages.
parts.why: Keeps the rocket pointed on the correct path to orbit.

parts.label: Reaction Control Thrusters
parts.realLocation: Small nozzles around the upper stage.
parts.why: Tiny thrusters that rotate the rocket while in space.

parts.label: Flight Computer
parts.realLocation: Mounted in the interstage electronics bay.
parts.why: Runs the whole flight and talks to the guidance system.

parts.label: Flight Battery
parts.realLocation: Next to the flight computer.
parts.why: Supplies power to guidance, avionics, and telemetry.

parts.label: Radio Telemetry
parts.realLocation: Mounted near the flight computer, with an antenna on the skin.
parts.why: Sends live speed and altitude data down to mission control.

parts.label: Upper-Stage Fuel Tank
parts.realLocation: Inside the second stage, above its engine.
parts.why: Holds the fuel that the second stage burns.

parts.label: Upper-Stage Oxidizer Tank
parts.realLocation: Inside the second stage, next to the fuel tank.
parts.why: Supplies oxygen so the upper-stage fuel can burn in space.

parts.label: Second-Stage Engine
parts.realLocation: At the bottom of the second stage.
parts.why: Restarts in space to finish the journey to orbit.

parts.label: Stage-Separation Camera
parts.realLocation: Mounted on the outside near the interstage.
parts.why: Films the exciting moment the two stages separate.

parts.label: First-Stage Fuel Tank (RP-1)
parts.realLocation: The large tank making up most of the first stage.
parts.why: Holds the kerosene-based fuel for the booster engine.

parts.label: First-Stage Oxidizer Tank (LOX)
parts.realLocation: The large tank next to the fuel tank in the first stage.
parts.why: Holds liquid oxygen so the booster can burn fuel.

parts.label: Heat Shield
parts.realLocation: Wrapped around the base of the booster.
parts.why: Protects the rocket from the intense heat of its own engine.

parts.label: First-Stage Booster Engine
parts.realLocation: At the very bottom of the rocket.
parts.why: The powerful main engine that lifts the whole rocket off the pad.

parts.label: Grid Fins
parts.realLocation: Near the top of the first stage, folded flat during ascent.
parts.why: Help steer the booster back down for a safe landing.

parts.label: Tracking Beacon
parts.realLocation: Mounted in the second-stage electronics bay, wired to the avionics battery. (Typical orbital rocket layout; exact position varies by model.)
parts.why: Sends a locating radio signal so ground teams can track the stage after separation or splashdown.
